speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Alexander Riseman on February 23, 1997.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the death of Pilsudski, things changed for the worse. Pilsudski was considered a friend of the Jews because-- that's what I heard. When he was an running away from the [INAUDIBLE] police, he was hiding himself from the [INAUDIBLE] police, he was hidden by a Jewish family. And later on, when Poland became independent, after 1918, he became the leader and he kept that it doesn't go to-- that anti-Semitism doesn't go too far. But after he died, the-- the fascists came to the fore. And I remember we lived in the center of the city and in Poland, on the 3rd of May is a national holiday. I don't know whether it still exists, this holiday, but before the war, it was a national holiday. And on the 1st of May was the workers' holiday, like we were socialists came up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the police used to on-- on-- on horses, they used to wait in the yard where we lived because they always expected trouble and they used to go out into the street to stop the riots and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always-- there were always fights. On the 3rd of May, we were always told not to go out into the street because there was always trouble. They used to pass the Jewish shops and-- and attack and break the windows and things like that, the fascists on the 3rd of May. And on the 1st of May, there was always fights between the socialists and the fascists. So we were also told not to go out. As children, we were told to keep indoors and this is the atmosphere that we were brought up. Coming back to the synagogues, as I said before, the-- the-- the three synagogues were the-- the most elaborate synagogues that one can imagine. They contained large libraries of very-- manuscripts, valuable manuscripts of different Talmudic learning. And one of the synagogues had two ladies' galleries-- two or three ladies' galleries. It could hold 1,500-- downstairs it had 1,500 seats. It was a very large synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it

speaker: interviewee
text: This was called the Old-- the Old Town Synagogue. And then there was the Vilna Shul that was the synagogue that was near us that was also a very large synagogue. And then there was a modern, more modern synagogue, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] founded by enlightened Jews. They had already an organ in the synagogue. There was a mixed choir. Something like the reform here, in England today. And they were the three largest synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the relationships between Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, as I said, was mainly Orthodox but most of the shops were closed on Shabbat, most of the Jewish shops, I'm talking about. Before the war, last years before the war broke out, some of the Jews started opening the shops on the Shabbat and the Hasidim used to stand outside the shops and stop the customers from going in and out of the shops.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a lot of-- and the-- the shop keepers used to call the police to intervene. They should move away these Hasidim So there was a lot of tension going.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on-- mainly, also the-- mainly the shops were-- the food shops I'm talking about, the kosher shops-- most of the things that were sold were kosher. But I remember it was-- must have been about 1936 or '37, somebody introduced a law into the Sejm, which is like the Parliament here, they should forbid the ritual slaughtering, the Shechitah. And as a protest, all Jews stopped buying meat or chickens between Pesach and Shavuot. That's a period of about six weeks. And the farmers, which were mainly Poles that used to bring the produce into town, suffered because a third of the population stopped buying meat. So-- also, funnily enough, the people that sold salt also suffered because Jews use salt to salt the meat before they eat it. You know, it's got to be salted. So they couldn't sell much-- the-- the-- the-- the sale of salt dropped because of the bill. So they also suffered. Finally, this bill was defeated and it never reappeared again. So also, I must say that some of the non-Orthodox Jews tried to buy meat from non-- from non-Jewish shops. And so the Jewish used to stand outside the non-Jewish shops and stop these people from going in to buy the non-kosher meat. And even the non-Orthodox Jews also stopped these people from going in because to-- they wanted this bill should be defeated as a matter of principle, even if they didn't care whether they have kosher meat or not. But to-- to know that to be solid-- to have solidarity, they also protested against this-- these people going into the other shops and that also helped for the bill to be defeated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe these-- these years in Poland until, say, 1930? Until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to me, this didn't affect me. I mean, I was a child. I was only in my-- well, I was 10 or-- or nine, depending on when. When the war broke out, I was 12 years old. So, as a child, I didn't take much notice of these things. Except sometimes they used to call me, children used to call me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], things like that, which is not a very nice compliment. So that's when I didn't like it but otherwise, it didn't bother me very much. But my people, grown-ups-- for instance, if a-- if a Jew wanted his son to start at university, he had to send him abroad because there was a quota in-- in how many Jews are allowed in Polish universities. So normally, a Jew, unless he was very rich, could not have his child, his son or his daughter study in a university unless he could afford to send them to abroad. Lots of things like that. For instance, a Jew could not open a business unless he-- first of all, every year you had to have, it was called a patent, such a certificate to open a shop or-- or a factory. And then you had to have-- see, every trade had a guild. You could not start a business unless you had the certificate from this particular guild. And these guilds, were-- a Jew could not normally get the certificate from such a guild, unless he employed a macher. A macher was called a man, a Jew, who knew somebody in the government that was connected with this particular guild and he bribed him and then he could get such a certificate to start a business. A lot of things like that. It was very hard to-- to start a business but somehow, we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever consider leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the '30s? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been about 1935, 1936, my father wanted to leave. First of all, he wanted to leave to Palestine but it was very hard to-- to get an exit visa from Poland before the war. You needed a passport. To get a passport, you had to pay a lot of money, also through a macher, these middlemen that arranged it with the government, with a government official. And then, to get into Palestine in those days was, as you know, there was a quota, I think, 1,500 or 15,000 per year people to enter Palestine, that's after the British government introduced the quota.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Palestine was out. So then my father wanted to go to South America, to the Argentine, he was considering to emigrate. So but the rabbi advised him against it because most of the people that went to these counties became non-religious. So that was all-- that was the reason why I didn't go to-- to-- why I didn't leave Poland before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also it was not so-- when a person lives in a country, born in a country, born in a town, and you-- you make a living, it's very hard to uproot yourself and to-- to go into the unknown and start all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the people carried on. A lot of people did emigrate but the majority didn't We didn't know what was waiting for us. So that's how it was. Did you feel at home in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my home, of course. I didn't know anything else. I didn't know anything else. It was my home and that I was amongst people, amongst Jews. Everything was Jewish and everything what you wanted, schools and libraries and everything. Used to have even people going around and the, the, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they used to go around in groups in yards, actors, you know, and perform plays in the yard and it was all in Yiddish. It was all-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- in a way, it was marvelous. For children, it was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you, when you were 10 and 11 years old, what did you want to become when you were grown up?

speaker: interviewee
text: A businessman, I suppose. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most Jews in Lodz were business people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not many had trades. Also, a lot of the trades, you couldn't enter. That's another thing, see? Jews could not enter all trades. Some places were forbidden to them. As I told you before, the guilds, they put a stop on the trades. Certain trades, if you bribe, you could get a certificate to start a business but in a lot of trades, even if you were prepared to bribe it didn't help. They wouldn't let in anybody, a Jew in such a-- it was definitely discrimination, no doubt about it, and it wasn't even hidden. It was open. It was taken for granted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- did you notice what was going on? Lodz is very near the German border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but we didn't-- we-- we-- I was-- we were with Germans all the time. I mean, we lived together with them but they were, as I said, ethnic Germans. They were Germans and they were already born in Poland but in the house, they spoke-- like we spoke Yiddish, they spoke German. And maybe they had some contact with Germany but most of them were Polish and they considered themselves, in a way, Poles, I suppose, up to a point, like we considered ourselves Poles, polish citizens. They considered themselves Polish citizens. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] In the late '30s, things-- after-- it must have been about 1936 I remember when Hitler came-- came to power after a year or two, he-- all the Jews that were of Polish-- a lot of Jews lived in Germany of Polish extraction, or Polish-Jewish extraction, and they were thrown out. The Nazis threw them onto the border between Poland and Germany. And at that time, we felt sorry for these people. In the papers, on the radio we heard that people being thrown out from their houses, from their businesses. And little did we realize what's waiting for us because eventually, these people, they managed to get away. Poland wouldn't let them in. So they were trapped on the border for months. But eventually, they managed to get visas to different countries and they emigrated to different countries. Lucky for them. If they would have stayed-- if Poland would have let them in, they would have ended up where we were ended up. So for them, it was good that they didn't, that Poland didn't let them in. That's how it worked out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this carried on till 1938 when Hitler marched into Austria and then Czechoslovakia. And then we realized that war is coming. But we were very heartened when England and France signed a Tripartite Pact with Poland, that if Poland is attacked, they will come to-- they will come to the Polish rescue. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't know. We thought that Hitler-- after this pact was signed, we-- we were hoping that Hitler will not risk and go to war if he's-- if England and France are going to defend Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, later on, I think it was in the beginning of 1939, they signed the pact with Russia and things deteriorated again. We lost-- we realized that war is unavoidable. It's-- it's going to happen but what could we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't go anywhere. We-- some people, if they were very wealthy, managed to get out, but most people couldn't. Even if they wanted to leave, they couldn't. It was-- also, nobody-- nobody allowed the Jews in. I mean, even if you managed to get over the border somewhere, they wouldn't let you in. They sent you back. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were trapped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean-- yes, we were about maybe a few hundred people, cousins and uncles and don't forget this-- the Jews lived in Poland for a thousand years, over a thousand years and you had relations all over the place, not necessarily in Lodz but whenever there was a wedding, they used to come. And we had a lot of people. Usually the weddings were very big. We used to-- to celebrate all night to the [INAUDIBLE] house and by the time we came out-- I remember once one of my aunts got married and all the relations descended on us. We had about 40 people sleeping in-- in our small apartment on the floor because one didn't have the money and it was unheard of to go and stay in a hotel like today. If people came, you just put them up. It was taken for granted. You gave them to eat and you gave them where to sleep. So that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did family members of you leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Some of them left for Russia in the beginning of the war. Well, I'm coming to that. It-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the war-- when-- a few weeks before the war broke out, we were still on holiday in the country, like I told you. We lived in the country and my father was-- it was he could see it's going to happen sooner or later so he managed to get a-- a wholesome car to take us back to-- to town. [DRINKS] And when we came back to Lodz, was already, the Polish government requisitioned all the modes of transport, horses and carriages and cars and taxis. Everything was requisitioned and there was already general mobilization. And we started digging trenches in the streets. I don't know what for but [LAUGHS] we were told to dig trenches. And they were giving-- they didn't have enough gas masks for the population. So they told us if we take, buy something like cotton candies and we dip it in bicarbonated soda and you put it on your face like the doctors wear in the hospital, such a mask, and this will protect us from a gas attack. So everybody started making up these artificial gas masks and we were told to put-- I suppose the same thing happened here-- on the windows to put the cross with sticky paper, white sticky paper for when the window shatter it shouldn't-- and also a lot of black material was being sold to cover over the windows, the light shouldn't show in the street. And we were getting ready for-- for war. And the Polish government, the radio was-- it was censored. It wasn't a free radio. So they were telling us how strong they are and how many tanks they have and how many planes they have. And that we shouldn't be frightened and the Germans don't stand a chance and they won't be able to defeat the Poles. The Poles are very strong, patriotic people and they will fight to the end and all kinds of-- and this went on for a few weeks until finally, on, as you know, on the 1st of September 1939, the Germans crossed over the border. And the radio was playing away and saying the Polish Army pounced the German tanks and they shot down so many German planes and they killed so many German soldiers and they-- they penetrated into German territory. And this went on for a few days. In the meantime, we had the air raids. And we were in-- they didn't have shelters, proper shelters, so what they did is they-- they collected a-- what they thought the most safe place in-- in the block in a basement and that's where we stayed all the time while the air raids went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: The air raids went on like this for-- from Friday to Monday. On Tuesday night, my father was a watchman. He was standing up on the roof seeing that if a fire breaks out to-- he was told what to do, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and there was an air raid on. So I didn't undress. I stood with my father on the roof. It was a three or four-story block. And we looked down onto the main square in-- off the Piotrkowska Street, which was the main street in Lodz. And as we looked down, we could see hundreds of Polish soldiers walking and some of them were bandaged, without shoes, without caps, torn uniforms. And we went down quick to find out what's going on. And we could hear-- they told us that the Germans had broken through the front line and they are-- and the Poles are retreating but we're not to tell anybody about it. But in the meantime, the radio was blaring away the victories, what they have achieved. [LAUGHS] It was in complete contradiction to what was going on on the ground. This was about 11 o'clock at night and after the air raid was over, we went into our apartment and we laid down. We've didn't undress but we laid down to have a few hours sleep. About 2 o'clock in the morning, there was a bang on the door, on our door and one of my uncles came in, and he said, get up, quick. Everybody's running away. The Germans are coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got up. We took everybody-- before the war broke out, everybody prepared themselves a rucksack with some main provisions, a bit of food and some clothes. But us children, we had a-- a bit of money on our-- in a little bag and a label on the-- with the name, the address and everything. So we put that on quickly and we run out into the street. And as we came into the street, already there were thousands and thousands of people. And the streets were packed and everybody was running. Nobody knew where they're running. It was chaos. Horses and cows and chickens, everything was in the-- in the middle of the street and everybody was running. Who were those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the inhabitants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, Poles, everybody was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans I don't think were running away. They-- they knew what was happening so they didn't run away but the Poles and the Jews were all running away. We would run-- we didn't know where we were running, away-- away from the Germans. The Germans were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we run like this till we came on the-- on-- outside the city, the suburbs. And the planes came down, the German planes came down and they started shooting into the crowds with machine guns. They could see-- they came very low. We could see the pilots even. And there was no-- the Poles, they hardly had any anti-aircraft guns. They were shooting with their rifles to try to shoot down the planes with their hand rifles. What choice did they have? So we were running like this for a few hours until we got tired. And then we heard that the Germans are approaching from all sides. So there was no point in running away. They would get us in any case. So my father decided to go back home. So we came back into our apartment and we-- we settled in and we prepared ourselves for the Germans to come in. But in the meantime, they shelled the city was heavy guns. So we had to sit in the shelters all the time while they were bombarding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we already prayed-- we, as children, we were terrified. We prayed already the Germans should come in because we couldn't take it anymore. The-- the-- the-- the-- the-- every time the shell came very close, exploded, it was unbearable. And this went on from Wednesday morning till Friday morning and on Friday morning, I was standing with my brother outside and we could see the German-- the-- the German cars approaching with the German flag and a swastika. And a few cars, they drove up to the main town hall, wasn't very far from us and they went inside. They were inside for about an hour. Then we could see the German flag with a swastika being hung out. They pulled down the Polish flag and put up the German flag. And then we knew that this is it. Germany has occupied Lodz.
